coredata.dc:title: Rational construction of fluorescent molecular imprinted polymers for highly efficient glycoprotein detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xiaoyu
Li, Jing
Zhen, Xueyan
Chen, Liang
Yuan, Wanqing
Feng, Quan
Liu, Xia
coredata.dc:description: The enrichment of glycoproteins by fluorescence molecular imprinting has shown an attractive application prospect, however, its specificity and sensitivity in actual sample analysis remain a significant challenge. In this work, a novel fluorescent molecular imprinted nanoplatform (MMIPs@QDs) was constructed for the efficient recognition and determination of glycoproteins by combining the glycopeptide template and the post-imprinting modification of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. The synthetic MMIPs@QDs exhibited high adsorption capacity and rapid binding kinetics. Satisfying selectivity and sensitivity were obtained using MMIPs@QDs, with an imprinting factor of 7.71 and a limit of detection of 0.20 μg mL−1, respectively. The efficiency and applicability of this proposed method were validated by the detection of ovalbumin in egg white samples, showed high recoveries (94.3%−100.2%) and acceptable relative standard deviation (1.79%−7.36%). This fluorescent molecular imprinted nanoplatform provides a new approach to address the limitations of existing protein detection techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent molecular imprinted polymers
Glycopeptide
Post-imprinting modification
Glycoprotein detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of template molecule on adsorption selectivity
Effect of pH on adsorption properties
Fluorescent response of MMIPs@QDs to OVA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82073807 ). We would like to thank Dr Jiamei Liu and Dr Ying Hao at the Instrument Analysis Center of Xi'an Jiaotong University for their assistance with XPS and FT-IR analysis.

This work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82073807). We would like to thank Dr Jiamei Liu and Dr Ying Hao at the Instrument Analysis Center of Xi'an Jiaotong University for their assistance with XPS and FT-IR analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of MMIPs@QDs.
Optimization of conditions for the preparation of MMIPs@QDs. (A) Functional monomer to cross-linker ratio; (B) The amount of template molecule; (C) Polymerization time; (D) Type of template.
TEM images of (A) Fe3O4, (B) MMIPs and (C) MMIPs@QDs.

(A) FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 (a), Fe3O4@SiO2 (b), Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA (c) and MMIPs@QDs (d); (B) XRD spectrum of Fe3O4 (a), Fe3O4@SiO2 (b), MMIPs (c) and MMIPs@QDs (d); (C) XPS patterns of Fe3O4 (a), Fe3O4@SiO2 (b), Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA (c) and MMIPs@QDs (d); (D) TGA curves of Fe3O4 (a), MMIPs (b) and MMIPs@QDs (c).
The investigation of adsorption performance. (A) Adsorption isotherms curves; (B) Adsorption kinetics curves; (C) Selectivity adsorption behaviors; (D) Competitive adsorption behaviors.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) MMIPs@QDs and (B) MNIPs@QDs.
Recoveries of OVA after MMIPs@QDs extracted of spiked fresh egg white (n = 3).
Comparison of protein detection methods based on different construction strategies of MMIPs@QDs.

head.ce:title: Rational construction of fluorescent molecular imprinted polymers for highly efficient glycoprotein detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Health Science Center, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Australian Centre for Research on Separation Science (ACROSS), School of Natural Sciences, University of Tasmania, Hobart, 7001, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Engineering Research Center of Cardiovascular Drugs Screening & Analysis, Xi'an, 710061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Engineering Research Center of Cardiovascular Drugs Screening & Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Engineering Research Center of Cardiovascular Drugs Screening & Analysis, Xi’an 710061, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Health Science Center, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710061, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The enrichment of glycoproteins by fluorescence molecular imprinting has shown an attractive application prospect, however, its specificity and sensitivity in actual sample analysis remain a significant challenge. In this work, a novel fluorescent molecular imprinted nanoplatform (MMIPs@QDs) was constructed for the efficient recognition and determination of glycoproteins by combining the glycopeptide template and the post-imprinting modification of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. The synthetic MMIPs@QDs exhibited high adsorption capacity and rapid binding kinetics. Satisfying selectivity and sensitivity were obtained using MMIPs@QDs, with an imprinting factor of 7.71 and a limit of detection of 0.20 μg mL−1, respectively. The efficiency and applicability of this proposed method were validated by the detection of ovalbumin in egg white samples, showed high recoveries (94.3%−100.2%) and acceptable relative standard deviation (1.79%−7.36%). This fluorescent molecular imprinted nanoplatform provides a new approach to address the limitations of existing protein detection techniques.

ce:section.ce:para: In eukaryotes, protein glycosylation is involved in many important life processes, including fertilization, development, metabolism, cellular recognition and signal transduction [1,2]. Abnormal glycoprotein expression is often accompanied by the occurrence of related diseases [3–5]. However, the low abundance of glycoproteins and their susceptibility to signal interference from the high abundance of non-glycoproteins make them particularly difficult to detect in complex biological systems [6,7]. Ovalbumin (OVA) is the most abundant glycoprotein in egg white and has been used as a representative glycoprotein in immunoassay and allergy research [8,9]. The traditional glycoprotein detection techniques, such as mass spectrometry [10,11], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [12,13] and antigen-antibody assay [14,15], have limitations of complex pretreatment, high cost and instability. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a highly sensitive and selective method for specific recognition and determination of target glycoproteins.

Fluorescent molecular imprinting polymers (FMIPs), formed by the combination of high selectivity of molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs) and high sensitivity of fluorescence detection, are promising for glycoproteins detection in complex biological samples [16–18]. However, the application of proteins in molecular imprinting remains a challenge due to their complex structure, flexible conformation and variability in organic solvents [19,20]. Recently, surface imprinting and doping methods have been proposed to develop FMIPs for glycoproteins detection [21,22]. In these methods, the imprinting layer was directly formed on the surface of fluorescent nanoparticles or created through the doping of fluorescent nanoparticles into imprinting polymers. The characteristic parts of template molecule [23,24], epitope imprinting [25,26] and mesoporous structure [27,28] have been successfully used to improve detection sensitivity, however, there are some issues in the construction of protein FMIPs. Firstly, although the applicability of template molecules can be improved by using a characteristic part of protein to serve as the template molecule, the selectivity of imprinting materials would be reduced. Secondly, the fluorescent nanoparticles were encapsulated in the polymers, making it difficult to achieve further enhancement of fluorescence intensity. Thirdly, after doping, imprinting polymerization and template molecular elution, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence nanoparticles was significantly quenched, resulting in a decrease in sensitivity. Therefore, the exploration of strategies to address these limitations is highly demanded for the improvement of detection sensitivity of protein FMIPs.

Generally, glycans or peptides, the characteristic fragments of glycoproteins, have been independently used as template molecules to synthesize FMIPs for glycoproteins detection [29,30]. Recently, glycopeptides, composed of glycans and peptides, have shown great potential as template molecules [31,32]. When utilizing glycopeptides as template molecules in molecular imprinting methods, the recognition is based on both glycans and peptides, thereby resulting in increased specificity. The post-imprinting modification strategy is another advantage for the construction of FMIPs. In contrast to the embedding method, the fluorescence signal in this technique is introduced after the elution of template molecules. In this way, an increased sensitivity of fluorescence sensing can be obtained as the fluorescence quenching caused by acidic eluent can be avoided and the contact distance between analytes and fluorescent materials can be reduced. Therefore, a multifunctional hybridization strategy combining glycopeptide template and post-imprinting modification is expected to improve the specificity and sensitivity of glycoproteins detection in complex sample matrix. To the best of our knowledge, few works based on accurate recognition of multiple characteristic fragments of template proteins and precise modification of fluorescent nanoparticles have been reported.

Herein, a novel strategy combining glycopeptide templates and post-imprinting modification of fluorescent nanoparticles was presented for highly specific and sensitive detection of glycoproteins. The Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were prepared as the magnetic core to provide a larger specific surface area and the rapid separation performance. Glycopeptide of OVA was used as a template molecule to generate two different specific recognition sites. After the elution of template molecules and cetrimonium bromide (CTAB), nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were applied to obtain magnetic and fluorescent biresponsive glycopeptide imprinted polymers (MMIPs@QDs) through post-imprinting modification. Subsequently, the MMIPs@QDs were characterized, and its adsorption and optical performances were investigated thoroughly. Additionally, the materials were evaluated by the determination of OVA in fresh egg white. This work opens up a promising approach to improve selectivity and sensitivity of FMIPS for the detection of proteins within complex samples.

OVA, bovine hemoglobin (BHb), lysozyme (Lyz), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and Pronase E were provided by Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and trypsin were obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O, trisodium citrate dihydrate, sodium acetate (CH3COONa), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and CTAB were purchased from Macklin Biochemical & Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zinc acrylate was supplied by J&K scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide sodium (NHS) and 4-formylphenylboronic acid (4-FPBA) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous toluene was purchased from CESHI Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shaanxi, China). Ultrapure water was supplied by an 1810B-XA water system (Chongqing, China). The fresh eggs were obtained from Glutenin fresh supermarket (Shaanxi, China).

The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 H R; Tokyo, Japan) was employed to characterize the morphologies of materials. Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) results were recorded by Micro-infrared spectroscopy (Bruker VERTEX70; Bremen, Germany). The X-Ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu LabX XRD-6100; Kyoto, Japan) was used to evaluate the crystal structure. The thermal stability results were depicted by using METTLER TOLEDO TGA/DSC3+ synchronous thermal analyzer (TGA, Greifensee, Switzerland). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB Xi+; Pittsburgh, USA) was used to assess the chemical composition. The magnetic properties were analyzed through vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM, MPMS-SQUID VSM-094; San Diego, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV) spectra were supplied by Fuli UV1600 spectrophotometer (Zhejiang, China). The fluorescence intensities were measured by SuPerMax-3000M2 Multifunctional microplate reader (Shanghai, China).

Fe3O4 MNPs were prepared by solvothermal method reported in literature [33]. In detail, FeCl3·6H2O (3.6 g) and sodium citrate (0.72 g) were added to ethylene glycol (100 mL) by ultrasonication for 2 h. Then, CH3COONa (4.8 g) was added to the solution under vigorous stirring and remained for 30 min in a water bath at 50 °C. Subsequently, the mixture was reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained Fe3O4 MNPs were washed sequentially with ultrapure water and absolute ethanol, followed by lyophilization in a lyophilizer.

In order to synthesize Fe3O4@SiO2, the absolute ethanol (80 mL) and ultrapure water (12 mL) were added to disperse Fe3O4 MNPs (100 mg) by ultrasonication for 30 min. After that, NH3·H2O (4 mL) and TEOS (200 μL) were added to the solution, and the mixture was reacted at 50 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the Fe3O4@SiO2 were modified in two steps to prepare the Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA. Firstly, Fe3O4@SiO2 (288 mg) was added to a mixture of anhydrous toluene (30 mL) and APTES (6 mL) for ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, followed by stirring in nitrogen atmosphere at 120 °C for 24 h to obtain Fe3O4@SiO2@APTES. Secondly, the obtained Fe3O4@SiO2@APTES (200 mg) and 4-FPBA (200 mg) were suspended in anhydrous methanol (20 mL) and reacted at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards, NaBH4 (50 mg) was added to the above suspension in every 4 h and repeated 5 times. Finally, the products were washed with water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA.

The preparation of MMIPs@QDs consisted of three steps, including the immobilization of short glycopeptide onto Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA, synthesis of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MMIPs) and post-imprinting modification of N-GQDs. For the immobilization of Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA with short glycopeptide, Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA (100 mg) was dispersed into 100 mM ammonium bicarbonate buffer containing 500 mM NaCl (10 mL, pH = 8.5) by ultrasonication. Then, 25 mg OVA was added to the suspension, followed by stirring at room temperature for 3h to obtain Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA fixed with intact OVA. The above precipitation was dispersed in a mixed solution of 100 mM ammonium bicarbonate buffer (10 mL, pH = 8.5) and 1 mg mL−1 trypsin (1 mL), and stirred at 37 °C for 3 h to obtain Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA immobilized with long glycopeptide. Subsequently, the resulting precipitate was scattered into 100 mM ammonium bicarbonate buffer (10 mL, pH = 8.5). Finally, 1 mg mL−1 Pronase E (1 mL) was added to the solution and reacted for 3h at 37 °C to obtain short glycopeptide of OVA immobilized Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA.

The preparation of MMIPs was performed by dispersing of short glycopeptide immobilized Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10 mL, pH = 8.5). Then, zinc acrylate (60 mg), MBA (45 mg) and CTAB (100 mg) were added to the solution and stirred at 70 °C for 12 h. In the next step, TEMED (100 μL) and APS (600 mg) were added to the upper suspension, and reacted at 70 °C for 6 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Finally, the obtained MMIPs were washed with methanol/acetic acid (8:2, v/v) to remove excess templates and dried at 60 C for 12 h.

N-GQDs were synthesized according to a previous study [34]. In brief, CA (0.21 g) and EDA (0.2 mL) were slowly added to ultrapure water (5 mL), and remained the mixture at 180 °C for 6 h for reaction. After cooling to room temperature, N-GQDs were obtained. Then, MMIPs (100 mg) were dispersed in ultrapure water (4 mL), followed by adding NHS (8 mL, 2 mg mL−1) to the solution and incubated for 30 min to activate the amino groups. After that, the synthesized N-GQDs (50 mL) were added to the above mixture and shacked for 4 h in the dark. Finally, the obtained MMIPs@QDs were washed with ultrapure water and collected by external magnetic field. As a control, magnetic non-imprinted polymers binding N-GQDs (MNIPs@QDs) were prepared in the same way except the addition of OVA.

To evaluate the rebinding properties of the obtained MMIPs@QDs on template proteins, static, dynamic, selective and competitive adsorption experiments were studied. The adsorption capacity of the material to OVA was assessed by calculating the fluorescence quenching efficiency (Eq) according to formula (1). The adsorption specificity of MMIPs@QDs was evaluated by imprinting factor (IF), as given in formula (2).
(1)
Eq = F 0/F－1
(2)
IF = Eq (MMIPs@QDs)/Eq (MNIPs@QDs)
where F 0 and F were the initial and equilibrium fluorescence intensities of MMIPs@QDs and MNIPs@QDs, respectively.

To perform the static adsorption experiment, 10 mg MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QDs were dispersed in a series of 10 mL OVA containing PBS (pH = 7.0) solutions, with the OVA concentrations ranged from 0 to 1.6 mg mL−1 and incubated at 25 °C for 20 min. The fluorescence intensities were recorded at initial and equilibrium stages, and the corresponding Eq and trends were calculated and estimated.

For the dynamic adsorption experiment, 10 mg MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QD were dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.0) containing OVA (0.4 mg mL−1). Then, the fluorescence intensity at different incubation time (0-120 min) was measured for the analysis of corresponding Eq and trends.

Under optimal conditions, the adsorption selectivity of MMIPs@QDs was evaluated. Briefly, MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QDs (10 mg) were dispersed in 0.4 mg mL−1 OVA, Lyz, HRP, BHb and BSA in PBS (10 mL, pH = 7.0), respectively. After that, the testing solutions were incubated for 20 min, and the fluorescence intensities were measured. In the competitive adsorption experiment, the mixtures of OVA and BSA with different ratios were used as analytes to incubate with MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QDs (10 mg). The Eq and relative standard deviation (RSD) values were evaluated.

The sensitivity experiment was studied to evaluate the quantitative fluorescence response of the obtained materials. For this purpose, 10 mg MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QDs were added to 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.0) containing different OVA concentrations, and the suspension was incubated at room temperature for 20 min. Then, the fluorescence intensities were recorded, and the linear relationships between Fo/F - 1 value and OVA concentration were plotted.

The stability and repeatability of materials are critical for practical applications. To evaluate these parameters, MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QDs (10 mg) were added in 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.0) under the light. The photo-stability was assessed by analyzing the change of fluorescence intensity at different time. Afterwards, MMIPs@QDs or MNIPs@QDs (10 mg), stored in dark for different days, were dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.0). The storage stability was analyzed by measuring the changes of fluorescence intensity. Finally, the recycling properties of the materials were investigated. MMIPs@QDs (10 mg) were dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.0) containing 0.4 mg mL−1 OVA. After rebinding for 20 min, the materials were collected by external magnetic field and eluted with methanol/acetic acid (8:2, v/v). The materials were subjected to 6 adsorption-desorption cycles, and the Eq of each adsorption process was determined.

To verify the applicability, MMIPs@QDs were applied for the detection of OVA in fresh eggs. For this purpose, MMIPs@QDs were dispersed in 100-fold diluted egg white samples using PBS (pH＝7.0). After incubation for 20 min, the solid precipitates were magnetically collected and then washed with 5% SDS-HCl (pH = 5.5) to eliminate interfering substances. Subsequently, the precipitates were magnetically collected again and eluted by methanol/acetic acid (8:2, v/v). The fluorescence intensities of the supernatant were measured, and the recovery and RSD values were calculated. For comparison purposes, the egg white samples were spiked with OVA at different concentrations, after which they were diluted 100-fold with PBS (pH＝7.0), with the final spiked concentrations at 0.70, 2.50 and 5.00 μg mL−1, respectively. Next, incubation, washing, elution and measurement procedures were carried out in sequence under the optimized conditions.

As demonstrated in Fig. 1 , Fe3O4 MNPs were covered with silica to act as magnetic response materials for the fast and convenient magnetic separation of them from samples. Glycopeptides were served as templates to generate glycan and peptide ends for dual recognition, resulted in the improvement of adsorption selectivity. N-GQDs, known as a superior fluorescent responsive material, were introduced through post-imprinting modification to avoid fluorescence quenching in acidic eluent, reduce the distance between OVA and fluorescent materials, and improve the sensitivity. After that, the obtained MMIPs@QDs were applied for specific recognition, rapid separation and sensitive detection of OVA within biological samples.

To obtain the most efficient imprinting sites, several parameters were optimized. Firstly, the ratio of functional monomer (zinc acrylate) to cross-linker (MBA) was studied as it would affect the degree of crosslinking of imprinted polymers and the adsorption performance of imprinted sites. As was shown in Fig. 2 A, the maximum Eq and IF values were obtained when the ratio of zinc acrylate to MBA was 1:1. Either a higher or a lower zinc acrylate to MBA ratio led to a decreased analytical performance. It is possibly that when the ratio was less than 1:1, excessive MBA would lead to a large degree of cross-linking. This could prevent the elution of template molecules, thus reducing the number of imprinting sites. While when the ratio was greater than 1:1, excessive zinc acrylate could result in increased non-specific adsorption. Therefore, the ratio of zinc acrylate to MBA at 1:1 was optimum for the preparation of MMIPs@QDs.

Secondly, the amount of template molecules (glycopeptides) was investigated. Glycopeptides fragments with a certain peptide length immobilized on boronate affinity sites were prepared as alternative templates by in situ dual enzymatic digestion [32]. OVA was used as glycan source for enzymatic digestion, which has the structure of N- glycans consisting of N-acetylglucosamine and mannose residues at the position of asparagine residues. As shown in Fig. 2B, the highest Eq and IF values were obtained with the weight ratio of Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA to OVA at 4:1. When the ratio was greater than 4:1, excessive Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA would lead to reduced Eq values of both MMIPs@QDs and MNIPs@QDs. When the ratio was less than 4:1, specific adsorption was reduced due to insufficient imprinting sites. Therefore, the weight ratio at 4:1 was selected for the preparation of MMIPs@QDs.

The polymerization time will affect the thickness of imprinted layer, which is critical for the adsorption properties of MMIPs@QDs. As depicted in Fig. 2C, the Eq and IF values were highest when the polymerization time was 6 h. Less polymerization time (3 h) was supposed to be not enough for the formation of sufficient specific adsorption sites. At the same time, long polymerization time (9 h) resulted in an overgrowth of imprinting layer, in which the templates are deeply embedded and thus making them difficult to be completely eluted. This led to decreased effective imprinting sites and reduced binding capacity.

MMIPs@QDs were formed by N-GQDs covalent bonding in the mesopore of MMIPs, and the amount of N-GQDs introduced was investigated. After dispersing 10 mg MMIPs in NHS, 3-6 mL N-GQDs were added, respectively. The obtained MMIPs@QDs were used to determine the adsorption capacity of OVA (0.4 mg mL−1, 10 mL, pH = 7.0). As shown in Fig. 1S, when the amount of N-GQDs was less than 5 mL, insufficient addition of fluorescent material reduced the fluorescence response sensitivity of MMIPs@QDs, resulting in a low Eq and IF values. When the amount of N-GQDs was greater than 5 mL, part of N-GQDs occupied the imprinted sites, resulting in a decrease in Eq and IF values. When the amount of N-GQDs was 5 mL, the Eq and IF values reached the maximum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of template molecule on adsorption selectivity
ce:section.ce:para: The template plays an important role in the specificity of an imprinted material. To demonstrate this, four materials (MMIPs@QDs, MNIPs@QDs, MMIPs@QDs-1 and MNIPs@QDs-1) were selected for the study of the effect of template molecules on the adsorption selectivity of imprinted materials. For this purpose, MMIPs@QDs-1 were synthesized using intact OVA without enzymatic hydrolysis as a template, and kept other conditions the same as the preparation of MMIPs@QDs as stated in Section 2.4. Meanwhile, MNIPs@QDs-1 were prepared according to e preparation conditions of MMIPs@QDs-1 without adding OVA. For the comparison purpose, 10 mg MMIPs@QDs, MNIPs@QDs, MMIPs@QDs-1 and MNIPs@QDs-1 were added to 10 mL OVA (0.4 mg mL−1) and incubated at room temperature for 20 min, respectively. After magnetic separation of solid adsorbent, the fluorescence intensities of supernatant were measured, and the Eq and IF values were calculated. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, MMIPs@QDs showed high Eq and IF values and were superior to MMIPs@QDs-1, indicating that MMIPs@QDs had good rebinding ability and specificity. This can be attributed to the lower mass transfer resistance of glycopeptides and the requirement of dual-recognition in adsorption sites. As expected, the Eq values of MNIPs@QDs-1 and MNIPs@QDs were much lower than those of imprinted materials, owing to the lack of imprinted sites. In summary, the application of glycopeptides as template molecules can increase the adsorption capacity due to their low mass transfer resistance, and enhance the binding specificity through the dual-recognition process.

The morphologies of Fe3O4, MMIPs and MMIPs@QDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 , Fe3O4 was composed of nanocrystalline clusters with spherical morphology, uniform size (150 nm) and good dispersion. After imprinting modification, a thin imprinting layer (10 nm) with rough surface was obtained (Fig. 3B) and the introduction of QDs did not break the imprinting layer (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, the magnetic properties of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and MMIPs@QDs were characterized by VSM (Fig. 2S). Although the magnetism of MMIPs@QDs was somewhat decreased due to the grafting of silica layer and imprinted layer, it remained sufficient to meet the requirements for magnetic separation.

To characterize the structure of the synthesized materials, FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA and MMIPs@QDs were recorded (Fig. 4 A). The vibration peak of Fe–O was observed at approximately 567 cm−1, demonstrating the successful synthesis of Fe3O4. The occurrence of peaks at 796 cm−1, 941 cm−1 and 1058 cm−1 assigned to Si–O, Si–O–H and Si–O–Si, respectively, indicating the successful bonding of silica layer on the surface of Fe3O4. The appearance of the vibration peak associated with B–O bond at 1390 cm−1 suggested the successful attachment of 4-FPBA. The successful preparation of MMIPs@QDs was verified by the observation of stretching vibration peaks at 1537 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, which were responsible for benzene ring and CO, respectively.

The XRD was employed to validate the crystallinity of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, MMIPs and MMIPs@QDs. As depicted in Fig. 4B, all materials possessed the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.3°, 35.5°, 43.4°, 53.7°, 57.2° and 62.9°, which were corresponding with the data (220, 311, 400, 422, 511 and 440) of Fe3O4 crystal in JCPDS No. 65-3107, respectively. The results indicated that Fe3O4 was successfully synthesized and no structural damage occurred after the surface functionalization and imprinting. The successful attachment of N-GQDs in MMIPs@QDs was verified by occurrence of characteristic peak of N-GQDs at 2θ = 25.4°, which was not seen in MMIPs.

The XPS was used to investigate the element compositions of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA and MMIPs@QDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, the characteristic peak of Fe 2p at 710 eV indicated that Fe3O4 was successfully prepared. The peak of Si 2p appeared at 103 eV, demonstrating that SiO2 was successfully coated on the surface of Fe3O4. The occurrence of B 1s and N 1s peaks confirmed the successful preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA. Compared with Fe3O4@SiO2@FPBA, the peak intensity of N 1s was increased, indicating the successful preparation of MMIPs@QDs.

The TGA curves for the clarification of thermal stability were presented in Fig. 4D. The weight of Fe3O4 was reduced approximately 13.41%, possibly due to the loss of carbon atoms of sodium citrate. After the temperature was increased to 340 °C, the weight of MMIPs decreased dramatically with a weight loss of 64.62%, which indicated that the prepared imprinting layer was damaged. Compared with MMIPs, more mass loss of MMIPs@QDs could be attributed to the decomposed of imprinted layer and the pyrolysis of N-GQDs. Demonstrating the successful synthesis of MMIPs@QDs.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of MMIPs@QDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3S, after rebinding with OVA, the fluorescence lifetime of MMIPs@QDs decreased from 10.63 ns to 9.99 ns. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of OVA for MMIPs@QDs were determined at 25 °C, 35 °C and 50 °C, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 4SA, the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased with increasing temperature and OVA concentration. The results suggested that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was a dynamic quenching process. A high temperature could facilitate the speed of molecular diffusion and collision, and thus increasing the fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 4SB, there was no spectral overlap between the UV spectrum of OVA and the fluorescence spectrum of MMIPs@QDs, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching was not originated from fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on adsorption properties
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that pH is a critical factor for the adsorption performance of boronic affinity-based imprinting materials. As shown in Fig. 5 S, the Eq and IF values of MMIPs@QDs were low when the pH was less than 7.0. This was possibly because the metal chelation and boronate affinity were inhibited under acidic condition. When the pH was greater than 7.0, the Eq and IF values of MMIPs@QDs decreased. It was mainly attributed to the electrostatic repulsion between OVA and negatively charged boric acid groups prevented the adsorption behavior, resulting in the reduced imprinting sites and weaker adsorption capacity of MMIPs@QDs. In short, rebinding and release could be adjusted by altering the pH of PBS, and pH = 7.0 was selected as the optimal rebinding conditions.

To better understand the adsorption performance of MMIPs@QDs to OVA, a series of experiments, including static adsorption, dynamic adsorption, selective experiments and competitive experiment were implemented.

To obtain the maximum adsorption capacity of the obtained materials, static adsorption tests were carried out. As depicted in Fig. 5A, the Eq value of MMIPs@QDs increased with OVA concentration over the range of 0–0.4 mg mL−1 and then achieved equilibrium from 0.4 mg mL−1 to 1.6 mg mL−1, reaching the maximum value at the OVA concentration of 0.4 mg mL−1. In comparison, when the OVA concentration was 0.1 mg mL−1, the Eq value of MNIPs@QDs reached the maximum, which was much lower than that of MMIPs@QDs. These results demonstrated that MMIPs@QDs delivered greater adsorption capacity than MNIPs@QDs. This is because the large number of imprinted sites on the surface of MMIPs@QDs can be matched to OVA through their unique chemical structures and configurations, while the attachment of OVA to MNIPs@QDs was primarily relied on non-specific interactions.

Dynamic adsorption experiment was carried out to investigate the adsorption efficiency. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of MMIPs@QDs decreased over time to 20 min and then reached equilibrium. A similar variation trend was seen in MNIPs@QDs, however, the fluorescence intensity of MNIPs@QDs was declined to a much lower level, and it took less time to achieve equilibrium. These results suggested that the imprinted sites formed on the surface of MMIPs@QDs contributed to good adsorption efficiency.

The specific recognition performances of MMIPs@QDs and MNIPs@QD were investigated through selectivity experiment, using OVA, HRP, Lyz, BHb and BSA as model molecules. As shown in Fig. 5C, MMIPs@QDs showed the highest Eq value for OVA amongst the five selected model chemicals. The unique binding ability of MMIPs@QDs to OVA was due to the presence of imprinted sites on the surface of MMIPs@QDs that matched well with the OVA in size and shape. In contrast, MNIPs@QD showed no significant difference in Eq for all the five model proteins because of the absence of specific imprinting sites on its surface. Furthermore, the corresponding IF of MMIPs@QDs to OVA, HRP, Lyz, BHb and BSA were 7.71, 3.00, 2.05, 1.91 and 1.44, respectively, indicating that the imprinted material had good selectivity for OVA.

The competitive experiments were applied to further assess the selective rebinding ability of MMIPs@QDs. As shown in Fig. 5D, the Eq value remained almost constant with the increase in BSA concentration. It demonstrated that MMIPs@QDs possessed satisfactory specificity for OVA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of MMIPs@QDs to OVA
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative response properties between MMIPs@QDs and OVA were further studied. The fluorescence intensity of both MMIPs@QDs (Fig. 6 A) and MNIPs@QDs (Fig. 6B) decreased with the increase in concentration of OVA and MMIPs@QDs showed a larger shift in fluorescence intensity in comparison with MNIPs@QDs. The possible reason was that the large number of imprinted sites existed in MMIPs@QDs could facilitate the adsorption of OVA, resulting in greater fluorescence quenching. The Fo/F - 1 value of MMIPs@QDs versus OVA concentration was linear in the range of 0.60–12.00 μg mL−1 (r = 0.9995). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to 0.20 μg mL−1 from 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement, and m is the slope of the linear calibration. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated to 0.67 μg mL−1.

The fluorescence stabilities of MMIPs@QDs and MNIPs@QDs were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 6SA, there were no observable fluorescence signal changes in both MMIPs@QDs and MNIPs@QDs, which indicated that both of them possessed good fluorescence stability over the measurement period. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 6SB, the fluorescence intensities of MMIPs@QDs and MNIPs@QDs were basically stable within 7 days, indicating that both of them delivered excellent storage stability.

Reusability is also an important factor for the evaluation of the practical application of a material. It could be seen from Fig. 7S, after six adsorption-desorption cycles, the Eq value of MMIPs@QDs was decreased from 0.284 to 0.225. A similar trend was observed for the Eq value of MMIPs@QDs, decreasing from 0.072 to 0.042. Although a slight decrease in Eq value of MMIPs@QDs occurred after the use for several times, presumably due to the incomplete elution of adsorbed protein or because of the damage imprinting sites in acidic condition. The fluorescence response of MMIPs@QDs remained sufficient for sensitive detection.

To assess the applicability and reliability of MMIPs@QDs, this material was applied for the analysis of fresh egg white samples. The concentration of OVA in fresh eggs was found to be 73 μg mL−1. As listed in Table 1 , the detection recoveries were ranged from 94.3% to 100.2% with RSD in the range of 1.79–7.36. The developed material is herein deemed suitable for the detection of OVA in real samples.

Additionally, the proposed method was compared with previous protein detection methods based on FMIPs with different construction strategies. The results were listed in Table 2 . Compared with other methods, the MMIPs@QDs based method delivered lower LOD and higher IF. The results demonstrated that the post modification strategy is a viable solution to improve detection sensitivity. The strategy also revealed that the simultaneous identification of glycan and peptide ends can enhance the selectivity of imprinted materials.

A novel magnetic and fluorescent biresponsive imprinted polymer platform was fabricated by combining the glycopeptide templates with the post-imprinting modification strategy for the enrichment and analysis of glycoprotein in complex samples. The developed MMIPs@QDs delivered good adsorption ability, high selectivity and sensitivity and satisfactory reusability. It also provided satisfactory recoveries for the detection of OVA in fresh egg white. More importantly, this research presented a new approach for the construction of protein-imprinting polymers. The specificity of the method was improved by the application of glycopeptides as the templates, in which both glycan and peptide ends were prerequisite for the dual recognition. This method also oﬀered the potential for nondestructive modification of fluorescent nanoparticles in the construction of FMIPs. Thus, this work supplies a new way for the construction of protein-imprinting polymers and has great potential for quantitative detection of target glycoprotein in complex biological samples.

Xiaoyu Xie: designed the experiment and wrote the manuscript. Jing Li: carried out the experiment and collected the data. Xueyan Zhen: supported the data. Liang Chen: revised the manuscript and analyzed the data. Wanqing Yuan: Analyzed the data. Quan Feng: Analyzed the data. Xia Liu: designed the experiment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82073807). We would like to thank Dr Jiamei Liu and Dr Ying Hao at the Instrument Analysis Center of Xi'an Jiaotong University for their assistance with XPS and FT-IR analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.339875.
